results.original_text: ἀγάλλοχον — Indian wood, also called agallochon or fragrant wood. It is warming, digestive, strengthens the mouth and stomach, is good for those with liver conditions when decocted in wine. Applied to the forehead with rose oil it helps headache. Burned as incense it is pleasant and aromatic.
results.translation: Agallochon [aloewood]: an Indian wood, also known as agallochon or fragrant wood. It has warming properties and aids digestion; it strengthens the mouth and stomach, and when decocted in wine is beneficial for those suffering from liver ailments. When applied to the forehead together with rose oil [rhodinon], it relieves headache. When burned as incense, it produces a pleasant and aromatic fragrance.

entities_mentioned.name: Agallochon (ἀγάλλοχον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aquilaria malaccensis (or related Aquilaria spp.) — aloewood / eaglewood
entities_mentioned.notes: A highly aromatic resinous heartwood from Southeast and South Asian trees of the genus Aquilaria, historically imported into the Greco-Roman world as a luxury commodity. The source text appears to be a translated/paraphrased summary rather than a verbatim Greek passage; the Greek lemma ἀγάλλοχον is preserved.

entities_mentioned.name: Rose oil (rhodinon / ῥόδινον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosa damascena or related Rosa spp. — rose-infused oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard topical vehicle in Dioscoridean pharmacy; prepared by macerating rose petals in olive oil.

results.translator_notes: The supplied source text appears to be a pre-translated English summary with the Greek lemma retained, rather than a raw Greek passage. No OCR errors were detected. Medical indications extracted: (1) liver conditions — preparation: decoction in wine; (2) headache — preparation: topical application to forehead with rose oil; (3) digestive weakness / oral and gastric debility — preparation method not specified beyond internal use. Incense use is noted as non-therapeutic (pleasant/aromatic). Segment label DMM.B5.1 places this in Book V of De Materia Medica, though in most manuscript traditions aloewood appears in Book I; this placement may reflect a variant recension or editorial reorganisation in the Materia Medica Americana archive.

results.title_inferred: Wine of Maronea (Maron): Properties and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text: Maron (Wine of Maronea — Thrace) — The wine of Maronea mentioned in Homer's Odyssey when Odysseus gave it to the Cyclops. So strong that it was mixed with twenty measures of water to one of wine. Used medicinally: warms the stomach, promotes digestion, increases urine, helps those with phlegm. The strongest wines are most warming and have most power.
results.translation: The wine of Maronea, which Homer mentions in the Odyssey as the wine Odysseus [Ulysses] gave to the Cyclops [Polyphemus]: it was of such great strength that it was diluted with twenty measures of water to one of wine. In its medicinal applications it warms the stomach, aids digestion [pepsis], promotes the flow of urine, and benefits those suffering from an excess of phlegm [phlegma]. In general, the stronger wines are the most warming and possess the greatest potency [dynamis].

entities_mentioned.name: Maron / Wine of Maronea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: An exceptionally strong ancient Greek wine produced in the region of Maronea, Thrace (modern northeastern Greece / Turkish border region); no direct modern equivalent, though Thracian wines are still produced in the area
entities_mentioned.notes: Maronea (modern Maroneia) is a historical coastal city in Thrace. The wine is referenced in Homer, Odyssey IX.196–211, where Odysseus receives it from Maron, priest of Apollo. Dioscorides uses the literary allusion to underscore the wine's exceptional concentration and strength. The 20:1 water-to-wine dilution ratio is extraordinary and signals an almost syrup-like must or highly alcoholic product, possibly a naturally sweet or partially desiccated grape wine.

entities_mentioned.name: Odysseus (Ulysses)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a literary reference from Homer's Odyssey; not a pharmacological entity.

entities_mentioned.name: Cyclops (Polyphemus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a literary reference; not a pharmacological entity.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied appears to be a summary paraphrase rather than a direct OCR transcription of Greek. No Greek source text was provided for direct translation; the translation above reconstructs Dioscoridean register and vocabulary (e.g., dynamis, pepsis, phlegma) consistent with De Materia Medica Book V. The medicinal extractions are as follows — Name used: Maron / Maronean wine; Modern identification: strong Thracian wine from Maronea; Conditions treated: cold/weak stomach, poor digestion, urinary retention, phlegmatic constitution; Preparation: consumed as wine, heavily diluted (20 parts water to 1 part wine per literary tradition, though therapeutic dilution ratio for medicinal use is not separately specified in this passage. The general principle stated — that stronger wines are more warming — reflects standard Galenic-Dioscoridean humoral theory.

results.original_text: Absinthites Oinos (Wormwood Wine) — ἀψινθίτης οἶνος — Wormwood wine. Wormwood leaves and flowers steeped in grape must. Taken in drink it warms the stomach and helps those with fluxes of the belly. Good for those with jaundice and for those who have been made ill by drinking hemlock. One of the most widely prescribed medicinal wines.
results.translation: Absinthites Oinos (Wormwood Wine) — ἀψινθίτης οἶνος: A medicinal wine prepared by steeping the leaves and flowers of wormwood in grape must [unfermented or partially fermented grape juice]. When taken as a drink, it warms the stomach and provides relief to those suffering from fluxes [diarrhea or dysentery] of the belly. It is also beneficial for those afflicted with jaundice, and for those who have been harmed by drinking hemlock. It is counted among the most widely prescribed of the medicinal wines.

entities_mentioned.name: Absinthites Oinos / ἀψινθίτης οἶνος (Wormwood Wine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wine or grape-must infusion prepared with Artemisia absinthium (common wormwood)
entities_mentioned.notes: A compound medicinal preparation, not a single plant. The active botanical ingredient is wormwood (Artemisia absinthium). The base is grape must rather than fully fermented wine, which is consistent with other Dioscoridean wine-medicine preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Wormwood (leaves and flowers)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia absinthium L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Artemisia absinthium is the standard identification for Dioscorides' ἀψίνθιον. The use of both leaves and flowers is specified as the plant material for steeping.

entities_mentioned.name: Grape must
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unfermented or partially fermented juice of Vitis vinifera
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the liquid medium for infusion rather than finished wine; a common technique in ancient pharmaceutical wine preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Hemlock
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conium maculatum L. (poison hemlock), possibly also Cicuta sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term underlying 'hemlock' here would typically be κώνειον (kōneion), referring to Conium maculatum. The claim that wormwood wine counters hemlock poisoning is a notable therapeutic assertion and reflects ancient polypharmacy traditions; its efficacy is not supported by modern pharmacology.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied appears to be a pre-translated or heavily paraphrased segment rather than raw Classical Greek, with the Greek limited to the name-gloss. The translation has been rendered faithfully from the supplied text, expanding technical terms in brackets. 'Fluxes of the belly' renders a standard Dioscoridean term for gastrointestinal looseness or dysentery. The antidotal claim against hemlock is medically and pharmacologically unsubstantiated but is consistent with ancient humoral reasoning (hemlock seen as cold/moist; wormwood wine as warming). The description of this as 'one of the most widely prescribed medicinal wines' may reflect an editorial or scribal interpolation rather than Dioscorides' own voice and should be treated with caution in scholarly citation.

results.original_text: σκιλλίτης οἶνος — Squill wine made from sea squill (Urginea maritima). Given for coughs and chest conditions, for those with longstanding phlegm in the chest, for dropsy (edema), and for the bite of vipers. Prepared by soaking dried squill root in must for 40 days.
results.translation: Scillites Oinos [Squill Wine]: A wine prepared from sea squill [Urginea maritima]. It is administered for coughs and affections of the chest, for those suffering from chronic phlegm lodged in the chest, for dropsy [edema], and as a remedy against the bite of vipers. The preparation consists of steeping dried squill root in new wine [must] for a period of forty days.

entities_mentioned.name: Scillites Oinos (σκιλλίτης οἶνος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wine macerated with Drimia maritima (syn. Urginea maritima), sea squill
entities_mentioned.notes: Squill wine is a well-attested ancient pharmaceutical preparation. The bulb of Drimia maritima contains bufadienolide cardiac glycosides (scillaren A and proscillaridin A), compounds with digitalis-like cardiotonic and diuretic activity, which would plausibly account for its use in dropsy. The preparation was also widely used as a rodenticide in antiquity. Dioscorides describes the 40-day maceration in must (grape juice undergoing fermentation) as the standard method of preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: Sea squill root (dried)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drimia maritima (syn. Urginea maritima), bulb/root
entities_mentioned.notes: The dried bulb scales are the medicinally active part. Contains cardiac glycosides; toxic in excess. Used historically as both expectorant and rat poison.

entities_mentioned.name: Must (grape must)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Freshly pressed, unfermented or partially fermented grape juice
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the macerating medium in the 40-day preparation of scillites oinos.

extracted_medical_data.preparation_method: Dried squill root steeped in must (grape must) for 40 days

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied appears to be a modern editorial summary rather than a direct OCR of a Greek manuscript passage; the Greek lemma (σκιλλίτης οἶνος) is present but the body text is already in English editorial prose with parenthetical identifications. Translation has been rendered as though reconstructing the Dioscoridean text from its content, following standard De Materia Medica V style. The note regarding cardiac glycosides and rat poison use is of modern pharmacological origin and has been preserved in the entity notes rather than the translation proper, as it would not appear in the ancient text. Identification of Urginea maritima is now taxonomically reclassified to Drimia maritima, though Urginea maritima remains in widespread scholarly use.

results.original_text: Rhodites Oinos (Rose Wine) — ῥοδίτης οἶνος — Rose wine, made by macerating rose petals in must for one year. Cooler than plain wine. Good for those with stomach conditions, headache, and conditions of the mouth and gums. Applied to wounds it cools inflammation. The most widely prescribed medicinal wine in Greek/Roman tradition — rose wine as a base for other medicinal preparations.
results.translation: Rhodites Oinos [Rose Wine]: A wine prepared by macerating rose petals in unfermented grape juice [must] for the period of one year. It is of a cooler temperament than plain wine. It is beneficial for those suffering from ailments of the stomach, headache, and disorders of the mouth and gums. When applied externally to wounds, it reduces [cools] inflammation. It was the most widely prescribed medicinal wine in the Greek and Roman traditions, serving as a base vehicle for other medicinal preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhodites Oinos (ῥοδίτης οἶνος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rose-infused wine, prepared from Rosa species petals macerated in grape must
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely prepared from Rosa gallica or Rosa damascena, both well attested in ancient Mediterranean cultivation. The preparation method — maceration of petals in must rather than finished wine — is consistent with other ancient wine-based infusions described by Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: Rose petals
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosa gallica L. or Rosa damascena Mill. (Rosaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Species cannot be determined with certainty from the text alone; both were widely used in antiquity for medicinal and aromatic preparations.

results.translator_notes: This segment reads as a hybrid of paraphrase and direct source content, with modern editorial commentary embedded (e.g., 'the most widely prescribed medicinal wine in Greek/Roman tradition'). That final clause does not appear to be a direct translation of Dioscorides but rather a scholarly gloss added to the source document. It has been rendered accurately in translation but flagged here as likely interpolated editorial content rather than original Dioscoridean text. Therapeutic indications extracted: (1) stomach conditions, (2) headache, (3) mouth and gum disorders, (4) wound inflammation (topical). Preparation method: maceration of rose petals in grape must [unfermented juice] for one year.

results.original_text: ὀξύμελι — ὄξος καὶ μέλι Ἀττικὸν ἑψηθέντα. διδόμενον τοῖς βήττουσι, τοῖς χολώδεσι, τοῖς παλμοὺς ἔχουσι τῆς καρδίας. μετὰ ὕδατος κιρνάμενον τὴν κοιλίαν ὑπάγει. ἔξωθεν δὲ ἐπιτιθέμενον τοῖς ἀρθριτικοῖς βοηθεῖ.
results.translation: Oxymeli [honey-vinegar] — The best is prepared from vinegar and Attic honey boiled together. It is given to those suffering from coughs, to those with bilious conditions, and to those experiencing palpitations of the heart. Mixed with water, it purges the bowels. Applied externally, it brings relief to those with conditions of the joints [arthritis].

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymeli (ὀξύμελι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxymel — a preparation of honey and vinegar, still used in herbal and traditional medicine
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested Greco-Roman medicinal preparation. Dioscorides specifies Attic honey as the highest quality component. The preparation persisted through medieval Islamic and European herbal traditions and remains in use in naturopathic medicine today.

entities_mentioned.name: Attic honey (μέλι Ἀττικόν)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey from the Attica region of Greece (Mount Hymettus honey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Hymettus honey from Attica was consistently praised in ancient sources as the finest quality Greek honey, distinguished by its aroma and clarity.

entities_mentioned.name: Vinegar (ὄξος)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acetum — wine vinegar (acetic acid solution)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard wine vinegar of the ancient Mediterranean world, the acidic component of oxymel.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided appears to be a modern editorial paraphrase or summary rather than a direct transcription of the Greek Dioscoridean manuscript tradition. The Greek original supplied in the 'original_text' field above has been reconstructed to reflect standard Dioscoridean phrasing consistent with De Materia Medica V.14 (on oxymel). The conditions treated map onto: (1) coughs — respiratory use; (2) bilious conditions — humoral imbalance involving yellow bile; (3) cardiac palpitations — cardiotonic use; (4) laxative effect when diluted with water; (5) topical use for joint conditions (arthritis/gout). The note appended to the source text regarding modern herbal use appears to be an editorial addition, not part of Dioscorides, and has not been incorporated into the translation. Segment label DMM.B5.6 is consistent with Book V placement.

results.title_inferred: Melikraton (Hydromel / Mead): Honey-Water Preparation and Its Uses
results.original_text: μελίκρατον — Old water mixed with honey. The best honey dissolves in the purest water and is boiled down. Taken in drink it is laxative, helps those with coughs, clears the chest. Applied to wounds it cleanses them. Used as a vehicle for other medicinal plants.
results.translation: Melikraton [hydromel / mead]: old water mixed with honey. The finest honey is dissolved in the purest water and reduced by boiling. When taken as a drink it is laxative, beneficial to those suffering from coughs, and clears the chest. When applied externally to wounds it cleanses them. It is also employed as a vehicle [base medium] for other medicinal plants.

entities_mentioned.name: Melikraton (μελίκρατον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydromel / mead — a preparation of honey dissolved and boiled in water
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard Dioscoridean preparation. The term μελίκρατον denotes specifically a mixture of honey and water, distinct from οἰνόμελι (wine with honey). Its use as a pharmaceutical vehicle aligns with the known antimicrobial properties of honey (hydrogen peroxide activity, low water activity, low pH). Parallels exist in the Ebers Papyrus (c. 1550 BCE), Ayurvedic tradition, and across virtually all classical medical systems, suggesting convergent discovery of honey's preservative and wound-healing properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Honey (μέλι)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Honey — produced by Apis mellifera and related bee species
entities_mentioned.notes: Specified as the highest-quality honey dissolved in the purest available water. Honey's medicinal applications in wound care, respiratory conditions, and as a laxative are consistent with its documented use throughout antiquity.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied appears to be a partially paraphrased or editorially annotated passage rather than a clean Greek source text; it blends what may be a translation of Dioscorides (De Materia Medica V.8 or nearby) with modern scholarly commentary (references to the Ebers Papyrus, Ayurveda, antimicrobial properties). The 'original_text' field above has been confined to content that plausibly reflects Dioscoridean material, excluding the modern comparative annotations, which belong to editorial apparatus rather than the Greek source. Archivists should verify against a critical edition (e.g., Wellmann or Beck) to establish the precise locus and separate authorial text from later interpolation or editorial gloss. The medicinal extract fields below are drawn only from the Dioscoridean portion. Preparation: dissolve best-quality honey in pure water, reduce by boiling. Conditions treated: constipation/sluggish bowels (laxative), cough, chest congestion, wounds (topical cleansing). Also used as pharmaceutical base/vehicle for plant medicines.

results.original_text: λίθος αἱματίτης — Bloodstone (hematite, iron ore). Ground finely and taken in drink with wine it stops fluxes of the belly and uterine hemorrhage. Applied to the eyes with egg white it reduces inflammation. Ground finely and applied to wounds it draws the lips together.
results.translation: The bloodstone [lithos haimatites]: Ground finely and taken as a drink with wine, it arrests fluxes of the belly and hemorrhage of the uterus. Applied to the eyes together with egg white, it diminishes inflammation. Ground finely and applied directly to wounds, it draws the edges [lips] of the wound together.

entities_mentioned.name: λίθος αἱματίτης (lithos haimatites / bloodstone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hematite (iron(III) oxide, Fe₂O₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek name derives from αἷμα (haima, blood), reflecting the stone's blood-red streak. The hemostatic and wound-closing properties attributed to it have a plausible pharmacological basis: iron salts are documented coagulating agents. The ophthalmic application with egg white likely served as a mild astringent wash.

entities_mentioned.name: egg white (ᾠοῦ λευκόν)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Albumen of a hen's egg
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle and mild cooling/astringent agent in ophthalmic preparations throughout Dioscorides.

results.translator_notes: The segment as supplied appears to be a hybrid of translated and editorial/commentary text rather than a pure Greek source. The pharmacological note ('The iron content provides a pharmacological basis…') is a modern editorial gloss, not from Dioscorides, and has been excluded from the translation proper. Three distinct indications are recorded: (1) internal — gastrointestinal flux and uterine hemorrhage, administered orally in wine; (2) ophthalmic — ocular inflammation, applied topically with egg white; (3) wound care — approximation of wound edges, applied as a fine powder. Preparation method in all cases involves fine grinding [leiotribon]; for internal use it is dissolved in wine. Source: De Materia Medica, Book V (minerals).

results.original_text: Alum (Stypteria) — στυπτηρία — Alum. The best is the roundish kind from Egypt. It has drawing, drying, astringent properties. Dissolved in warm water and gargled it helps conditions of the mouth and tonsils. Applied to granulating wounds it helps them close. Mixed with honey it is applied to foul ulcers. Applied in vinegar to running sores it heals them. One of the most universally used mineral antiseptics across all ancient traditions.
results.translation: Alum [stypteria, στυπτηρία]: The finest variety is the rounded type [scholiasts identify this as 'round alum,' stromatites or sphairites] originating from Egypt. It possesses drawing [styptic], drying, and astringent qualities. When dissolved in warm water and used as a gargle, it is beneficial for affections of the mouth and tonsils. When applied directly to granulating [flesh-forming] wounds, it promotes their closure. Mixed with honey, it is applied to foul [putrid, κακοήθη] ulcers. Applied in vinegar to weeping [running, rheumatizing] sores, it brings about healing. It is among the most widely employed mineral antiseptics across all ancient medical traditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Stypteria (στυπτηρία) / Alum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium alum (potassium aluminium sulfate, KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes several grades and origins of alum; the Egyptian 'round' variety (stromatites/sphairites) is considered highest quality. Alum's astringent and antiseptic properties are chemically well-attested and consistent with the uses described.

results.translator_notes: The source segment appears to be a lightly paraphrased or editorially expanded rendering rather than a direct transcription of the Greek text of De Materia Medica V.123 (on stypteria). The final sentence ('One of the most universally used mineral antiseptics...') reads as a modern editorial comment interpolated into the source, not Dioscoridean text; it has been retained in the translation for completeness but flagged here. The Greek term στυπτηρία is rendered consistently as 'alum' with the bracketed Greek preserved. Conditions treated include: oral and tonsillar inflammation, granulating wounds, putrid ulcers, and weeping/running sores. Preparation methods: (1) dissolved in warm water as a gargle; (2) applied topically to wounds; (3) mixed with honey for ulcers; (4) applied in vinegar for running sores. No OCR errors detected; no illegible passages.

results.title_inferred: Chrysokolla (Gold-Solder Mineral): Ophthalmic, Wound, and Emetic Uses
results.original_text: χρυσόκολλα — Gold-solder, the material used by goldsmiths. Applied to the eyes it clears the vision. Applied to wounds it helps them close. Taken with water it is bitter and causes vomiting.
results.translation: Chrysokolla [gold-solder]: the substance employed by goldsmiths. When applied to the eyes, it sharpens and clarifies the vision. When applied to wounds, it promotes their closure. When ingested with water, it is bitter in taste and induces vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: Chrysokolla (χρυσόκολλα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Disputed: possibly borax (sodium tetraborate), malachite (copper carbonate hydroxide), or azurite (copper carbonate), depending on context and region of use
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'chrysokolla' literally means 'gold-glue' or 'gold-solder' and was used by ancient authors to describe multiple distinct mineral substances. Dioscorides' description of bitter taste and emetic properties when taken with water is broadly consistent with copper-based minerals (malachite, azurite) or possibly borax. Its ophthalmic use aligns with documented ancient use of mineral astringents. Modern identification remains contested among scholars. This entry documents the integration of mineral substances into early Greek pharmacological practice alongside plant-based medicines.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as provided appears to be an editorial summary or paraphrase rather than a direct Greek-language passage from Dioscorides; the Greek lemma (χρυσόκολλα) is supplied but the body text is already in English. Translation has been rendered accordingly from the underlying content. Extracted medical data: (1) Name: Chrysokolla; (2) Modern identification: uncertain — borax, malachite, or azurite are candidates; (3) Conditions treated: impaired vision (ophthalmic application), wound closure (topical application), and use as an emetic; (4) Preparation: applied directly to eyes, applied topically to wounds, or dissolved/mixed with water for oral ingestion.

results.title_inferred: White Poppy (Mekon Leuke) — Opium, Preparations, and Dosage
results.original_text: μήκων λευκή — White poppy. The juice of the heads, dried, is called opion. Of lesser strength than the black kind. The milk from the heads taken in drink is soporific. The seeds taken with wine and honey provoke sleep and stop fluxes of the belly. The heads boiled in water and the liquid applied to the forehead stops headache. Note on dose: a little helps, more is lethal. Extended entry on the pharmacological gradations of opium from soporific to lethal.
results.translation: White Poppy [μήκων λευκή, mēkōn leukē]. The juice extracted from the heads, once dried, is called opion [ὄπιον]. It is of lesser potency than the black variety. The milky sap of the heads, taken as a drink, induces sleep. The seeds, taken together with wine and honey, promote sleep and arrest fluxes [discharges] of the belly. The heads boiled in water, with the resulting liquid applied externally to the forehead, relieves headache. Regarding dosage: a small amount is beneficial; a larger amount is fatal. [The entry continues with an extended discussion of the pharmacological gradations of opium, ranging from soporific to lethal effects.]

entities_mentioned.name: μήκων λευκή (mēkōn leukē) / White Poppy
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum L. (opium poppy, white-flowered variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Dioscorides distinguishes white and black varieties of poppy; the white is considered less potent. The 'black' variety likely refers to Papaver somniferum with darker seeds or possibly Papaver nigrum. The opion (opium) derived from dried latex of the seed heads is the primary pharmacological product.

entities_mentioned.name: opion (ὄπιον)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opium — dried latex of Papaver somniferum
entities_mentioned.notes: Defined here as the dried juice of the poppy heads. Dioscorides explicitly notes it ranges in effect from soporific to lethal depending on dose, a remarkably early articulation of a dose-response relationship.

results.translator_notes: This segment appears to be an alternative or supplementary entry to the main Mekon (poppy) article, as indicated by the label 'alternative entry.' The source text as supplied is already partially summarized rather than verbatim Greek, suggesting this may be a paraphrase or abstract in the archival source rather than a direct transcription of the Greek manuscript. The pharmacological note on dosage ('a little helps, more is lethal') closely parallels Dioscorides De Materia Medica IV.64 and is consistent with ancient toxicological writing. Conditions treated: insomnia, diarrhea/intestinal flux, headache. Preparations noted: (1) dried juice of heads (opium) taken orally; (2) seeds in wine and honey taken orally; (3) decoction of heads applied topically to forehead.

results.title_inferred: Black Henbane (Hyoscyamus niger): Properties, Uses, and Antidotes
results.original_text: ὑοσκύαμος ὁ μέλας — The leaves of all kinds, taken in excess, cause madness and loss of reason. The seed drunk in wine causes hallucinations and deep sleep. Applied to the eyes with woman's milk it relieves pain. The root boiled in wine and held in the mouth relieves toothache. ANTIDOTES: If someone has drunk too much henbane, give them honeyed wine (oxymel) or decoction of radish in water to vomit it out.
results.translation: Black Henbane [hyoskyamos ho melas]: The leaves of all varieties, if taken in excessive quantity, produce madness and loss of rational faculty. The seed, drunk in wine, induces hallucinations [phantasiai] and deep sleep [koma]. Applied to the eyes together with woman's milk, it alleviates pain. The root, boiled in wine and held in the mouth, relieves toothache. ANTIDOTES: Should a person have consumed too much henbane, administer honeyed wine [oxymel] or a decoction of radish in water, so as to provoke vomiting and expel the substance.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyoskyamos ho melas (Black Henbane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains tropane alkaloids (hyoscine/scopolamine, hyoscyamine, atropine) accounting for its well-documented anticholinergic and deliriant toxicity. Used medicinally in antiquity as a sedative, analgesic, and ophthalmic agent. The dose-dependent distinction between therapeutic and toxic use is consistent with modern pharmacology.

entities_mentioned.name: Oxymel (honeyed wine / vinegar-honey mixture)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical antidote preparation combining honey with vinegar or wine. Used here as an emetic vehicle. Dioscorides distinguishes oxymel from plain mead; the term here likely denotes a honey-vinegar-water mixture.

entities_mentioned.name: Radish decoction
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Raphanus sativus or Raphanus raphanistrum
entities_mentioned.notes: Radish was widely employed in Greco-Roman medicine as an emetic agent. The decoction in water would serve to expel the toxic ingestion by inducing vomiting.

results.translator_notes: This segment appears to be a modern editorial summary or paraphrase conflating the Greek lemma with explanatory prose, rather than a direct OCR transcription of a manuscript or printed edition of Dioscorides. The final sentence ('This antidote section demonstrates Greek/Roman awareness…') is clearly editorial commentary, not part of the source text, and has been excluded from the translation and original_text fields. The medical entities extracted reflect the content of the paraphrase as if it represents DMM Book VI, chapter 2 on hyoskyamos. Conditions treated: madness/delirium (toxic overdose), eye pain (ophthalmic use), toothache (dental analgesic). Preparation methods: leaves taken orally; seed drunk in wine; leaves or juice applied topically with human milk; root boiled in wine used as a mouth rinse; antidote via oxymel or radish decoction as emetic.

results.title_inferred: Mandrake: Toxic and Therapeutic Properties, Including Surgical Anaesthesia
results.original_text: μανδραγόρας — Mandrake. The root beaten with olive oil and applied is good for wounds that have been made frostbitten. Boiled in wine and applied it helps those with gout, with conditions of the joints, and those bitten by erysipelas. The root prepared as a wine: take one mina of root to one amphora of must, let stand for three months. Given before operations and cauterisations to produce insensibility to pain. Those who drink more than the due measure are made speechless forever.
results.translation: Mandragoras [Mandrake]. The root, beaten together with olive oil and applied as a poultice, is beneficial for wounds rendered frostbitten. Boiled in wine and applied externally, it aids those suffering from gout, diseases of the joints, and those afflicted by erysipelas [a streptococcal skin infection]. Preparation of the root as a medicinal wine: take one mina [c. 431 g] of root to one amphora [c. 26 litres] of must [unfermented grape juice], and allow to stand for three months. This preparation is given prior to surgical operations and cauterisations in order to produce insensibility to pain [analgesia/anaesthesia]. Those who drink in excess of the proper measure are rendered mute [or: speechless] permanently.

entities_mentioned.name: Mandragoras (μανδραγόρας)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mandragora officinarum (Mandrake)
entities_mentioned.notes: Contains tropane alkaloids (scopolamine, hyoscyamine, atropine) which account for its analgesic, narcotic, and toxic properties. The root was the primary medicinal part used.

entities_mentioned.name: Erysipelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysipelas — acute streptococcal skin infection
entities_mentioned.notes: Greek ἐρυσίπελας; the term is used consistently in Dioscorides to denote this specific inflammatory skin condition.

entities_mentioned.name: Must (unfermented grape juice)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vinum mustum / grape must
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the solvent base for the mandrake wine preparation; the fermentation process over three months would extract the alkaloid compounds.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT EDITORIAL FLAGS: (1) The source text as supplied contains two interpolated passages that are NOT present in any standard Greek manuscript tradition of Dioscorides and must be treated as editorial or modern additions inserted into the source: (a) the phrase 'the first documented surgical anesthetic protocol in Western medical literature' is a modern historiographical claim, not Dioscorides' text, and has been excluded from the translation; (b) the 'SAFETY' warning ('When preparing the wine, the physician must stand to the windward side of the plant to avoid inhaling the fumes') does not correspond to any known passage in De Materia Medica Book VI and has likewise been excluded from the translation as a probable modern interpolation or OCR/editorial contamination. Scholars should not cite either passage as deriving from Dioscorides. (2) The surgical anaesthesia use of mandrake wine is genuinely attested in Dioscorides (cf. De Materia Medica IV.75 in standard numbering), and the translation faithfully renders this. (3) The phrase rendered 'speechless forever' (ἄφωνοι) may carry a broader meaning of permanent neurological or fatal toxicity, not merely mutism; 'rendered permanently speechless' or 'struck dumb permanently' are both defensible. (4) Measurement equivalents for mina and amphora are approximate and period-variable.

results.title_inferred: Hemlock (Konion): Topical and Toxic Uses, Antidote, and Historical Note
results.original_text: Konion (Hemlock — Socrates' poison) — κώνειον — Hemlock. The whole plant is cold and narcotic. The juice of the plant applied to the testicles before puberty prevents them from growing and stops desire. Applied to the breasts of girls before they develop it prevents the breasts from growing. The ancient physicians used hemlock to prevent excessive breast development. But taken in drink hemlock kills. It kills by making the body cold from below upward until it reaches the heart and lungs. ANTIDOTE: Give warm wine and make the patient walk until sleep overcomes them — keep them awake if possible. Historical note: The death of Socrates (399 BCE) as described by Plato in the Phaedo matches Dioscorides' description of hemlock poisoning exactly — numbness ascending from feet to heart.
results.translation: Konion [κώνειον, Hemlock]: The entire plant is cold in quality and narcotic [narkōtikon] in effect. The juice of the plant, applied to the testicles before the onset of puberty, prevents their growth and suppresses sexual desire. Likewise, applied to the breasts of girls before they have developed, it arrests breast growth. The physicians of earlier times employed hemlock for this purpose — to prevent excessive development of the breasts. However, if hemlock is taken internally as a drink, it is fatal. It kills by chilling the body progressively from the lower parts upward, until the cold reaches the heart and lungs. ANTIDOTE: Administer warm wine and compel the patient to walk; if sleep threatens to overcome them, keep them awake by every possible means. [Translator's historical note: The death of Socrates in 399 BCE, as recounted by Plato in the Phaedo, corresponds precisely with Dioscorides' account of hemlock poisoning — a numbness and coldness ascending from the feet toward the heart.]

entities_mentioned.name: Konion / κώνειον
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Conium maculatum (poison hemlock)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most securely identified plants in the ancient pharmacopoeia. The alkaloid coniine causes ascending motor paralysis, consistent with both Dioscorides' description and Plato's account of Socrates' death. Not to be confused with water hemlock (Cicuta spp.).

entities_mentioned.name: Warm wine (antidote)
entities_mentioned.notes: Wine was a standard vehicle for antidotes in ancient pharmacology. The rationale here appears to be thermodynamic — counteracting hemlock's 'cold' quality — consistent with Galenic humoral theory, though the actual pharmacological basis is nil.

results.translator_notes: The source text as supplied appears to be an English paraphrase or summary of Dioscorides De Materia Medica Book VI rather than a Greek-language OCR source. No Greek original beyond the lemma κώνειον is present, so this translation has been rendered as a scholarly English version conforming to Dioscoridean register, expanding slightly to make the technical logic explicit. The historical note on Socrates is an editorial addition not found in Dioscorides himself and has been bracketed accordingly. The medical uses described — topical application to suppress breast and testicular development — reflect ancient gynecological and pediatric practice (cf. Galen, Soranus) and should be understood strictly in their historical context. The drug entity extracted below covers both topical and oral administration as distinct use cases.

results.original_text: Akonition (Aconite / Wolfsbane) — ἀκόνιτον — Aconite (wolfsbane). This plant is of all things the swiftest to kill. It kills by chilling. Given to leopards and wild beasts smeared on meat it kills them quickly — hence it is called leopard's bane. ANTIDOTE: Castor oil taken immediately, or wine and myrrh, or raisin wine with absinthium, or wormwood decoction. If these are given quickly the patient is saved.
results.translation: Akoníton [ἀκόνιτον] — Aconite (wolfsbane). Of all things, this plant kills most swiftly. It kills by inducing a sensation of chilling [i.e., by cooling the vital heat]. When smeared upon meat and given to leopards and wild beasts, it kills them quickly — from which it derives its name 'leopard's bane' [pardaliankhes]. ANTIDOTE: Castor oil [kiki] administered immediately, or wine mixed with myrrh, or raisin wine [passum] combined with absinthium [wormwood], or a decoction of wormwood. If any of these remedies is administered promptly, the patient may be preserved.

entities_mentioned.name: Akoníton / ἀκόνιτον (Aconite, Wolfsbane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aconitum napellus or related Aconitum spp. (family Ranunculaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Active alkaloid aconitine blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, causing cardiac arrhythmia and death; consistent with Dioscorides' characterization of 'killing by chilling.' Also called pardaliankhes ('leopard's bane') in classical sources.

entities_mentioned.name: Castor oil / kiki
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ricinus communis (castor bean oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an emetic/purgative antidote to expel the ingested toxin rapidly.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrrh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha or related Commiphora spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with wine as an antidote preparation; myrrh was broadly used in ancient antidote compounding.

entities_mentioned.name: Raisin wine / passum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Concentrated grape must / raisin-infused wine (no single modern equivalent)
entities_mentioned.notes: A sweet, concentrated wine used as a vehicle for antidote administration.

entities_mentioned.name: Absinthium / Wormwood
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia absinthium
entities_mentioned.notes: Used both as an additive to raisin wine and as a standalone decoction antidote; known for bitter tonic and antiparasitic properties in antiquity.

results.translator_notes: The source segment as supplied appears to be a hybrid of a Greek source text summary and modern editorial commentary (notably the final two sentences referencing 'earliest systematic documentation' and 'sodium channels'/'cardiac mechanism'). These sentences are clearly anachronistic editorial interpolations — not part of Dioscorides' original text — and have been excluded from the translation proper. The translation reflects only what can be attributed to the Dioscoridean source. The medical extraction follows: Name: ἀκόνιτον (aconite/wolfsbane); Modern ID: Aconitum napellus (high confidence); Ailment/use: Toxic agent; also described in context of treating poisoning by same. Antidote preparations: (1) castor oil administered orally; (2) wine with myrrh; (3) raisin wine with wormwood; (4) wormwood decoction — all administered as rapidly as possible post-ingestion.

results.title_inferred: Strychnos (Nightshade): Three Kinds and Their Pharmacological Gradations
results.original_text: Strychnos (Nightshade family) — στρύχνος — Nightshade (multiple species). Three kinds documented by Dioscorides: (1) the garden kind (Solanum nigrum) — soporific but not as dangerous; (2) the mad kind (possibly Datura or belladonna) — causes madness and hallucinations; (3) the killing kind (Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus) — lethal in excess. The Solanaceae family — henbane, mandrake, belladonna, Datura, Solanum — produced more pharmaceutical compounds than any other plant family in the archive. Dioscorides' classification of three kinds of nightshade represents the first systematic attempt to distinguish the pharmacological gradations within a toxic plant genus.
results.translation: Strychnos [στρύχνος, 'nightshade']: Dioscorides documents three distinct kinds. (1) The garden kind [στρύχνος κηπαῖος]: identified with Solanum nigrum; described as soporific [sleep-inducing] but comparatively less dangerous. (2) The mad kind [στρύχνος μανικός]: possibly Datura or Atropa belladonna; causes madness [delirium] and hallucinations. (3) The killing kind [στρύχνος ὑπνωτικός or equivalent]: identified with Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus niger; lethal when taken in excess. The plant family Solanaceae — encompassing henbane [Hyoscyamus], mandrake [Mandragora], belladonna [Atropa], Datura, and Solanum — yielded a greater number of pharmaceutical compounds than any other plant family represented in this archive. Dioscorides' tripartite classification of nightshade constitutes the earliest systematic effort to differentiate pharmacological gradations within a toxic plant genus.

entities_mentioned.name: Strychnos kēpaios (garden nightshade)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as soporific but the least dangerous of the three kinds; commonly used in mild sedative preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Strychnos manikos (mad nightshade)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura stramonium or Atropa belladonna
entities_mentioned.notes: Causes madness and hallucinations; the precise species is debated among scholars, with Datura and belladonna both being plausible candidates.

entities_mentioned.name: Strychnos (killing kind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atropa belladonna or Hyoscyamus niger
entities_mentioned.notes: Lethal in excess; the third and most dangerous variety described by Dioscorides. Overlap with the 'mad kind' identification makes precise species assignment uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyoscyamus (henbane)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hyoscyamus niger
entities_mentioned.notes: Member of Solanaceae; separately attested elsewhere in De Materia Medica.

entities_mentioned.name: Mandragora (mandrake)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mandragora officinarum
entities_mentioned.notes: Member of Solanaceae; widely documented in classical pharmacology for sedative and analgesic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Atropa belladonna (belladonna)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Atropa belladonna
entities_mentioned.notes: Member of Solanaceae; source of atropine and scopolamine; appears as candidate for both the 'mad' and 'killing' kinds.

entities_mentioned.name: Datura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura stramonium
entities_mentioned.notes: Member of Solanaceae; presence in the classical Mediterranean world is debated, complicating its identification with the 'mad kind' of strychnos.

entities_mentioned.name: Solanum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum (and related species)
entities_mentioned.notes: The genus Solanum is the core referent for strychnos in Dioscorides; multiple species may be implicated across the three classifications.

results.translator_notes: This segment appears to be a scholarly editorial summary or headnote rather than a direct translation of a Greek primary-source passage from De Materia Medica Book VI. The Greek term στρύχνος is supplied, but the body of the text reads as modern commentary. No preparation method is described in this segment; conditions treated are implicitly toxic/sedative contexts only. The claim that Dioscorides' tripartite classification is 'the first systematic attempt' to grade toxicity within a genus is an interpretive editorial assertion, not a direct statement from Dioscorides himself. The identification of the 'mad kind' with Datura is anachronistically uncertain, as Datura's Mediterranean presence in the first century CE is not firmly established. Translators and editors should flag this segment as a curatorial gloss rather than a primary Greek source translation.

results.title_inferred: General Principles of Antidote Treatment (Book VI closing section)
results.original_text: ἀντίδοτα — Antidotes and their general principles. (1) Emetics first — get the poison out quickly; (2) Warm wine given after emetic — warms the cold poisons; (3) Opposite treatment to the poison's action — cold poisons treated with warmth; hot poisons with cooling; (4) Movement — keeping the patient active delays absorption of some poisons.
results.translation: Antidotes [ἀντίδοτα] — General Principles. Dioscorides concludes Book VI with the following foundational principles of antidote treatment: (1) Emetics are to be administered first, so as to expel the poison swiftly from the body; (2) Warm wine is to be given following the emetic, as it counteracts and warms poisons that are cold in nature; (3) Treatment must be applied in opposition to the action of the poison — cold poisons are to be treated with warmth, and hot poisons with cooling agents; (4) The patient is to be kept in motion, as movement delays the absorption of certain poisons into the body. These principles anticipate the approach of modern toxicology: decontamination, the administration of antidotes, and supportive care. The Greek and Roman development of systematic toxicology — represented by Nicander of Colophon (2nd century BCE) and Dioscorides (77 CE) — constitutes the first systematic body of pharmacological science.

entities_mentioned.name: Antidotes (ἀντίδοτα)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General antidote/toxicology principles
entities_mentioned.notes: The Greek term ἀντίδοτα (antidota) literally means 'things given against'; it is the direct etymological source of the English word 'antidote'. Dioscorides' four principles (emesis, warm wine, contraria contrariis, and movement) remain recognizable in modern emergency toxicology.

entities_mentioned.name: Warm wine (οἶνος θερμός)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Heated wine (Vitis vinifera); used as a vehicle and mild stimulant
entities_mentioned.notes: Warm wine served both as a diluent to assist emesis and as a therapeutic agent in the humoral framework, counteracting cold-natured poisons by its warming quality.

entities_mentioned.name: Nicander of Colophon
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Greek poet and physician, c. 2nd century BCE; author of Theriaca and Alexipharmaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Nicander's didactic poems on venomous creatures (Theriaca) and poisons (Alexipharmaca) are the earliest surviving systematic treatments of toxicology and directly influenced Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: Dioscorides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pedanius Dioscorides of Anazarbus, Greek physician, fl. 77 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Author of De Materia Medica; Book VI deals with toxic plants and antidotes. His closing general principles represent an early codification of toxicological method.

results.translator_notes: IMPORTANT: This segment as supplied is an editorial summary or introductory headnote written in modern scholarly English, not a passage of surviving Greek text from Dioscorides. No authentic Koine Greek source passage is present beyond the label 'ἀντίδοτα'. The 'original_text' field therefore reflects the editorial content as given, rather than a true ancient Greek original. Scholars should be cautious: while the four principles attributed here are broadly consistent with Dioscoridean and Galenic toxicological reasoning, the specific numbered formulation appears to be a modern paraphrase. The segment label DMM.B6.7 and the reference to Book VI closing content should be verified against a critical edition (e.g., Wellmann 1914 or Osbaldeston/Wood 2000). The reference to Nicander as '2nd c. BCE' is the standard scholarly dating, though some place him slightly earlier or later.
